title: Roosevelt Island
content:
Roosevelt Island is a narrow island in New York City's East River, within the borough of Manhattan. It lies between Manhattan Island to its west and the borough of Queens, on Long Island, to its east. Running from the equivalent of East 46th to 85th Streets on Manhattan Island, it is about long, with a maximum width of, and a total area of.

Together with Mill Rock, Roosevelt Island constitutes Manhattan's Census Tract 238, which has a land area of, and had a population of 11,661 as of the 2010 United States Census. Lying below the Queensboro Bridge, the island cannot be accessed directly from the bridge itself.

Vehicular traffic uses the Roosevelt Island Bridge to access the island from Astoria, Queens, though the island is not designed for vehicular traffic and has several areas designed as car-free zones. Several public transportation options to reach the island exist. The Roosevelt Island Tramway, the oldest urban commuter tramway in the U.S, connects the island to Manhattan Island's Upper East Side.

The Roosevelt Island station carries the F and <F trains</a of the <a href="New%20York%20City%20Subway" New York City Subway</a. The <a href="NYC%20Ferry" NYC Ferry</a also maintains a dock on the east side of the island.

On-island transport is provided by the <a href="Roosevelt%20Island%20Red%20Bus" Red Bus</a service. The island was called by the <a href="Lenape" Lenape</a and Varkens Eylandt (Hog Island) by <a href="New%20Netherlander" New Netherlander</a s, and during the colonial era and later as Blackwells Island.

It was known as Welfare Island when it was used principally for hospitals, from 1921 to 1973. It was renamed Roosevelt Island (in honor of <a href="Franklin%20D.%20Roosevelt" Franklin D. Roosevelt</a) in 1973.

Roosevelt Island is owned by the city but was leased to the <a href="New%20York%20State%20Urban%20Development%20Corporation" New York State Urban Development Corporation</a for 99 years in 1969. Most of the residential buildings on Roosevelt Island are rental buildings.

There is also a <a href="Housing%20cooperative" cooperative</a named Rivercross and a condominium building named Riverwalk. One rental building (Eastwood) has left New York State's <a href="Mitchell-Lama%20Housing%20Program" Mitchell-Lama Housing Program</a, though current residents are still protected. It is now called Roosevelt Landings.

There are attempts to privatize three other buildings, including the cooperative. The <a href="FDNY" FDNY</a also maintains its Special Operations Command facility at 750 Main St. on the island. History. 17th and 18th centuries.

In 1637, Dutch Governor <a href="Wouter%20van%20Twiller" Wouter van Twiller</a purchased the island, then known as Hog Island, from the <a href="Canarsie%20Indians" Canarsie Indians</a.

After the Dutch surrendered to the English in 1664, Captain John Manning acquired the island in 1666, which became known as Manning's Island, and twenty years later, Manning's son-in-law, Robert Blackwell, became the island's new owner and namesake.

In 1796, Blackwell's great-grandson Jacob Blackwell constructed the <a href="Blackwell%20House" Blackwell House</a, which is the island's oldest landmark, <a href="List%20of%20the%20oldest%20buildings%20in%20New%20York" New York City's sixth oldest house</a, and one of the city's few remaining examples of 18th-century architecture. 19th century.

Through the 19th century, the island housed several hospitals and a prison. In 1828, the City of New York purchased the island for $32,000 (), and four years later, the city erected a <a href="prison" penitentiary</a on the island; the Penitentiary Hospital was built to serve the needs of the prison inmates.

By 1839, the <a href="New%20York%20City%20Lunatic%20Asylum" New York City Lunatic Asylum</a opened, including the <a href="The%20Octagon%20%28Roosevelt%20Island%29" Octagon Tower</a, which still stands but as a residential building; it was renovated and reopened in April 2006.

The asylum, which was designed by <a href="Alexander%20Jackson%20Davis" Alexander Jackson Davis</a, at one point held 1,700 inmates, twice its designed capacity. In 1852, a <a href="workhouse" workhouse</a was built on the island to hold petty violators in 220 cells.

The <a href="Smallpox%20Hospital" Smallpox Hospital</a, designed by <a href="James%20Renwick%2C%20Jr." James Renwick, Jr.</a, opened in 1856, and two years later, the Asylum burned down and was rebuilt on the same site; Penitentiary Hospital was destroyed in the same fire.

In 1861, prisoners completed construction of Renwick's <a href="City%20Hospital%20%28Roosevelt%20Island%29" City Hospital</a (renamed Charity Hospital in 1870), which served both prisoners and New York City's poorer population. In 1877, the hospital opened a School of Nursing, the fourth such training institution in the nation.

During the impeachment process of <a href="State%20Supreme%20Court" New York State Supreme Court</a Justice <a href="George%20G.%20Barnard" George G. Barnard</a in 1872, the first charge that the New York City Bar Association brought against Barnard was that he discharged at least 39 prisoners from the Blackwell's Island penitentiary before their sentence was expired.
